name: palmares-republic-zumbi
friendly_name: Palmares Republic - African Resistance in Brazil
description: Escaped enslaved Africans established Palmares, the largest quilombo in Brazil, resisting Portuguese colonial rule for nearly a century.

name: tongan-kingdom-established
description: The Tuʻi Kanokupolu dynasty consolidated power in Tonga, creating a unified Polynesian kingdom that would endure for centuries.

name: mapuche-confederation-1610
description: Mapuche peoples of southern Chile formed political alliances to resist Spanish colonization, maintaining independence for centuries.

name: stock-exchange-amsterdam
description: The Amsterdam Stock Exchange opened as the world's first formal securities exchange, trading shares of the Dutch East India Company.

name: shogun-tokugawa-succession
description: After the Siege of Osaka, Tokugawa Ieyasu eliminated rival claimants and secured hereditary shogunal succession for his dynasty.

name: beaver-trade-north-america
friendly_name: Beaver Fur Trade in North America
description: European demand for beaver pelts drove extensive trade networks with Indigenous peoples, reshaping North American economies and alliances.

name: richelieu-centralization
description: Cardinal Richelieu became chief minister to Louis XIII, consolidating royal authority and weakening the French nobility and Huguenots.

name: dahomey-kingdom-founded
description: The Kingdom of Dahomey was founded in present-day Benin, growing into a powerful West African state with a formidable military.

name: mughal-shah-jahan
friendly_name: Shah Jahan's Reign and Taj Mahal
description: Shah Jahan ascended the Mughal throne, presiding over an era of architectural splendor including the construction of the Taj Mahal.

name: alaouite-dynasty-rise
description: The Alaouite dynasty began its rise to power in Morocco, eventually establishing the ruling dynasty that continues to the present day.

name: gondar-capital
description: Emperor Fasilides established Gondar as the capital of Ethiopia, building grand castles and churches that still stand today.

name: buke-shohatto-code
description: The Tokugawa shogunate issued the Laws for Military Houses, regulating daimyo behavior and reinforcing centralized feudal control.

name: stock-speculation-dutch
description: Dutch tulip mania peaked and collapsed in 1637, often considered the first recorded speculative bubble in financial history.

name: tokugawa-sakoku-isolation
friendly_name: Tokugawa Sakoku Policy of Isolation Begins
description: Japan's Tokugawa shogunate began issuing sakoku edicts restricting foreign trade and banning Japanese from traveling abroad.

name: sakoku-edicts-isolation
description: The final sakoku edict expelled the Portuguese and sealed Japan's borders, limiting foreign contact to a Dutch trading post in Nagasaki.

name: fronde-rebellions
description: French nobles and parliamentarians revolted against royal authority during the young Louis XIV's minority, lasting until 1653.

name: westphalia-treaty
description: Treaties ending the Thirty Years' War established the principle of state sovereignty and reshaped the European political order.

name: peace-westphalia-extended
description: Westphalia's territorial settlements redrew European borders, granting Sweden and France key territories and confirming Dutch independence.

name: mercantilism-doctrine-rise
description: European states increasingly adopted mercantilist policies favoring exports, trade monopolies, and colonial resource extraction.

name: futures-market
description: The Dojima Rice Exchange in Osaka became the world's first organized futures market, standardizing forward contracts for rice trading.

name: vietnamese-nam-vu
friendly_name: Vietnamese Nam Vu (March to South) Expansion
description: Vietnamese settlers pushed steadily southward into Cham and Khmer territories, expanding the Nguyen lords' domain toward the Mekong Delta.

name: zanzibar-spice-trade
description: Zanzibar emerged as a major Indian Ocean spice trading hub under Omani influence, exporting cloves, nutmeg, and cinnamon.

name: navigation-acts-england
description: England's Navigation Acts required colonial trade to be carried on English ships, establishing a mercantilist framework for the British Empire.

name: koprululer-reforms
description: Grand Vizier Köprülü Mehmed Pasha launched sweeping Ottoman reforms, restoring military discipline and administrative efficiency.

name: mughal-aurengzeb-expansion
description: Aurangzeb seized the Mughal throne, expanding the empire to its greatest territorial extent across the Indian subcontinent.

name: english-civil-war-aftermath
description: The Restoration of Charles II in 1660 ended the republican Commonwealth, but Parliament retained significant powers won during the civil wars.

name: louis-xiv-personal-rule
description: After Mazarin's death, Louis XIV declared he would rule without a chief minister, beginning his era of absolute monarchy.

name: qing-kangxi-emperor
description: The Kangxi Emperor ascended the Qing throne at age seven, eventually becoming one of China's longest-reigning and most capable rulers.

name: maratha-shivaji
description: Shivaji Bhonsle was crowned Chhatrapati, formally establishing the Maratha kingdom as an independent Hindu power against Mughal dominance.

name: central-bank
description: The Riksbank was established in Stockholm as the world's first central bank, tasked with lending and maintaining monetary stability.

name: maratha-empire-begins
description: Shivaji's coronation as Chhatrapati launched the Maratha Empire's expansion across western and central India.

name: rozvi-empire
description: The Rozvi Empire emerged in present-day Zimbabwe after overthrowing Mutapa, dominating the region through military and trade networks.

name: mercantilism-peak
description: European mercantilism reached its height under Colbert in France, with states aggressively pursuing trade surpluses and colonial monopolies.

name: joseon-yangban-examination
description: Joseon Korea's yangban elite dominated civil service examinations, reinforcing a hereditary scholar-official ruling class.

name: ottoman-decline-begins
description: The failed Ottoman siege of Vienna in 1683 marked a turning point, beginning a long period of territorial retreat in Europe.

name: locke-treatise-government
description: John Locke published his Two Treatises of Government, arguing for natural rights and government by consent of the governed.

name: english-rights-act
description: Parliament enacted the Bill of Rights after the Glorious Revolution, limiting royal power and guaranteeing parliamentary supremacy.

name: mongolia-qing-vassalage
friendly_name: Mongolia Becomes Qing Dynasty Vassal State
description: Outer Mongolian Khalkha leaders submitted to Qing authority at the Dolon Nor assembly, becoming vassals of the Chinese empire.

name: khalkha-mongolia-submission
description: Threatened by the Dzungar Khanate, Khalkha Mongolian princes formally submitted to Qing Emperor Kangxi at the Convention of Dolon Nor.

name: tonga-empire
description: The Tongan maritime empire reached its peak influence, with political and trade networks extending across the western Pacific islands.

name: zanzibar-sultanate-influence
description: Omani rulers expanded Zanzibar's influence along the East African coast, controlling trade in ivory, spices, and enslaved people.

name: dahomey-rise-power
friendly_name: Dahomey Kingdom Rise to Regional Power
description: Under King Agaja's predecessors, Dahomey expanded militarily in West Africa, building a centralized state with a powerful army.

name: tlingit-shellmoney-trade
description: The Tlingit people of the Pacific Northwest used dentalium shell currency in extensive trade networks spanning coastal North America.

name: famine-cycles-break
friendly_name: Famine Cycles Break in Western Europe
description: Agricultural improvements and New World crops helped break the recurring cycle of devastating famines in Western Europe.

name: asante-confederation
description: Osei Tutu united Akan states under the Golden Stool, forming the powerful Asante Confederation in present-day Ghana.

name: mughal-administrative-decline
description: After Aurangzeb's death in 1707, the Mughal Empire fragmented rapidly as provincial governors became increasingly autonomous.

name: copyright-britain
description: The Statute of Anne became the world's first copyright law, granting authors exclusive rights to their works for a limited period.

name: haitian-slave-rebellions
friendly_name: Early Haitian Slave Rebellions and Marronage
description: Enslaved people in Saint-Domingue staged uprisings and fled to mountain communities, laying groundwork for later revolution.

name: mississippi-bubble
description: John Law's Mississippi Company scheme in France collapsed spectacularly, ruining investors and destabilizing French state finances.

name: qing-tibet-control
friendly_name: Qing Dynasty Establishes Control Over Tibet
description: Qing forces expelled Dzungar invaders from Lhasa and installed a new Dalai Lama, establishing Chinese suzerainty over Tibet.

name: maratha-confederacy
description: The Peshwa assumed effective control and Maratha chiefs formed a confederacy, becoming the dominant power across much of India.

name: yongzheng-emperor-reign
description: The Yongzheng Emperor succeeded Kangxi, implementing efficient tax reforms and centralizing Qing administrative power.

name: stono-rebellion
friendly_name: Stono Rebellion - South Carolina Slave Uprising
description: Enslaved Africans near Charleston staged the largest slave revolt in British colonial America, killing over 20 whites before suppression.

name: separation-of-powers-concept
description: Montesquieu published The Spirit of the Laws, articulating the separation of powers into legislative, executive, and judicial branches.

name: qing-expansion-central-asia
friendly_name: Qing Expansion Reaches Peak in Central Asia
description: The Qing dynasty conquered Dzungaria and the Tarim Basin, bringing Xinjiang under Chinese control at the empire's greatest extent.

name: maratha-expansion-peak
description: The Maratha Confederacy reached its greatest territorial extent, controlling much of the Indian subcontinent before defeat at Panipat.

name: building-code
description: Hammurabi's code included early building regulations in Babylon, holding builders liable for structural failures with severe penalties.

name: middle-passage-peak
friendly_name: Middle Passage Atlantic Slave Trade Peak
description: The transatlantic slave trade reached peak volume in the mid-18th century, with millions of Africans forcibly transported to the Americas.

name: triangular-trade-system
description: The Atlantic triangular trade peaked, exchanging European goods for enslaved Africans and colonial raw materials like sugar and tobacco.

name: dahomey-slave-trade-dominance
friendly_name: Dahomey Dominates West African Slave Trade
description: The Kingdom of Dahomey became a major supplier of enslaved captives to European traders through the port of Ouidah.

name: british-museum-founded
description: The British Museum was established by an Act of Parliament, becoming one of the world's first public national museums.

name: industrial-revolution-begins
description: Britain's transition from agrarian to industrial economy began, driven by textile mechanization, coal power, and factory production.

name: tacky-rebellion-jamaica
description: Tacky, an enslaved Akan chief, led a major slave revolt in Jamaica that took months for the British to suppress.

name: proclamation-1763
description: Britain prohibited colonial settlement west of the Appalachian Mountains after the French and Indian War, angering American colonists.

name: public-education-prussia
description: Frederick the Great mandated compulsory primary education in Prussia, establishing one of the world's first state-run school systems.

name: hermitage-museum
description: Catherine the Great founded the Hermitage in St. Petersburg by acquiring a major art collection, now one of the world's largest museums.

name: factory-system-emergence
description: Richard Arkwright opened the Cromford Mill, pioneering the factory system of centralized water-powered textile production.

name: intolerable-acts
description: Britain passed the Coercive Acts to punish Massachusetts after the Boston Tea Party, pushing American colonies toward revolution.

name: first-continental-congress
description: Delegates from twelve colonies met in Philadelphia to coordinate a response to British policies, establishing colonial unity.

name: boulton-watt-partnership
description: Matthew Boulton and James Watt partnered to manufacture improved steam engines, powering the Industrial Revolution's expansion.

name: declaration-of-independence-drafted
description: Thomas Jefferson drafted and the Continental Congress adopted the Declaration, proclaiming American independence from Britain.

name: manchester-cotton-boom
description: Lancashire's cotton mills expanded rapidly with mechanized spinning, making the region the center of the global textile industry.

name: pre-revolution-debt-crisis
description: France faced near-bankruptcy from war debts and lavish spending, forcing Louis XVI to convene the Assembly of Notables.

name: sotho-kingdom-mohlomi
description: Chief Mohlomi laid the foundations for the Sotho kingdom in southern Africa through diplomacy and alliance-building.

name: american-constitution-adoption
description: The United States Constitution was ratified by the required nine states, establishing the federal government framework still in use today.

name: storming-bastille-france
description: Parisians stormed the Bastille fortress on July 14, becoming the defining symbol of the French Revolution's outbreak.

name: estates-general-convened
description: Louis XVI convened the Estates-General at Versailles for the first time since 1614, triggering the events of the French Revolution.

name: declaration-rights-man-citizen
friendly_name: Declaration of Rights of Man and Citizen
description: The National Assembly adopted this foundational document of human rights, declaring liberty, equality, and popular sovereignty.

name: womens-march-versailles
description: Thousands of Parisian women marched to Versailles over bread shortages, forcing the royal family to return to Paris.

name: abolition-of-feudalism
description: The National Assembly abolished feudal privileges on August 4, dismantling centuries of aristocratic rights in France.

name: prison-reform
description: Philadelphia's Walnut Street Jail pioneered the penitentiary model focused on solitary confinement and rehabilitation over punishment.

name: flight-to-varennes
description: Louis XVI and Marie Antoinette attempted to flee France but were captured at Varennes, fatally undermining the constitutional monarchy.

name: september-massacres
description: Parisian mobs killed over a thousand prisoners in September 1792, fearing a royalist counter-revolution as foreign armies advanced.

name: abolition-of-monarchy
description: The National Convention abolished the French monarchy and declared the First French Republic on September 21, 1792.

name: reign-terror-french-revolution
description: The Committee of Public Safety under Robespierre executed thousands of perceived enemies through revolutionary tribunals.

name: execution-of-louis-xvi
description: Louis XVI was guillotined in Paris on January 21, 1793, after being convicted of treason by the National Convention.

name: louvre-opened
description: The Louvre opened as a public museum during the French Revolution, displaying royal art collections for all citizens.

name: thermidorian-reaction
description: Robespierre was overthrown and executed in the Thermidorian Reaction, ending the Reign of Terror and the radical phase of the Revolution.

name: directory-period
description: The French Directory took power as a moderate republican government, ruling France until Napoleon's coup in 1799.

name: hawaiian-kingdom-unification
description: Kamehameha I unified the Hawaiian Islands through military conquest, establishing a centralized Pacific kingdom.

name: income-tax
description: Britain introduced the first modern income tax under William Pitt the Younger to fund the Napoleonic Wars.

name: kwakwakawakw-potlatch
description: The Kwakwaka'wakw of the Pacific Northwest held elaborate potlatch ceremonies redistributing wealth and reinforcing social hierarchies.

name: sikh-empire-ranjit-singh
description: Ranjit Singh captured Lahore and founded the Sikh Empire, unifying Punjab into a powerful independent state in South Asia.

name: abolition-slavery-denmark
description: Denmark became the first European country to enforce a ban on the transatlantic slave trade, enacted in 1792 and effective from 1803.

name: napoleon-coronation
description: Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of the French at Notre-Dame Cathedral in the presence of Pope Pius VII.

name: criminal-law-code
description: Napoleon enacted the Civil Code, a comprehensive legal system that influenced law across Europe and former French colonies.

name: continental-system
description: Napoleon imposed a trade blockade against Britain through the Berlin Decree, attempting to cripple the British economy.

name: divorce-law
description: Revolutionary France legalized divorce in 1792, becoming one of the first modern states to allow dissolution of marriage by mutual consent.

name: restoration-bourbon-monarchy
description: After Napoleon's abdication, the Bourbon dynasty was restored with Louis XVIII taking the French throne.

name: zulu-kingdom-formation
description: Shaka Zulu unified Nguni clans into the powerful Zulu Kingdom, revolutionizing warfare tactics in southern Africa.

name: museum-prado-opened
description: The Prado Museum opened in Madrid, housing the Spanish royal art collection including masterworks by Velazquez and Goya.

name: mfecane-populations-displaced
description: The Mfecane upheaval caused massive population displacement across southern Africa as Zulu expansion triggered chain migrations.

name: police-force-modern
description: Sir Robert Peel established London's Metropolitan Police, creating the model for modern professional law enforcement.

name: child-labor-law
description: Britain's Factory Act of 1833 prohibited child labor under age nine and limited working hours, a landmark in labor regulation.

name: female-university
description: Mount Holyoke Seminary opened in Massachusetts as one of the first institutions of higher education for women in the United States.

name: pension-system
description: Bismarck introduced the world's first state pension system in Germany, providing old-age insurance for workers over 70.

name: dental-school
description: The Baltimore College of Dental Surgery opened as the world's first dental school, professionalizing the field of dentistry.

name: womens-rights-convention
description: The Seneca Falls Convention launched the American women's suffrage movement, producing the Declaration of Sentiments.

name: universal-suffrage
description: France adopted universal male suffrage during the 1848 Revolution, granting all adult men the right to vote.

name: public-health
description: Britain's Public Health Act established local boards of health to improve sanitation and combat cholera epidemics.

name: sanitation-system
description: Britain created the General Board of Health to oversee water supply and sewage systems in response to urban disease outbreaks.

name: detective-agency
description: Allan Pinkerton founded the Pinkerton National Detective Agency in Chicago, pioneering private law enforcement in America.

name: library-system
description: The Boston Public Library was established as one of the first large free municipal public libraries in the United States.

name: ethiopian-tewodros-reform
description: Emperor Tewodros II reunified Ethiopia and attempted to modernize the state, ending the Era of Princes.

name: nursing-school
description: Florence Nightingale established the first professional nursing school at St Thomas' Hospital in London.

name: womens-property-rights
description: Britain's Married Women's Property Act of 1870 allowed married women to own and control their own earnings and property.

name: information-bureau
description: Austria established an early tourist information bureau, helping organize the growing phenomenon of leisure travel.

name: scramble-africa-begins
description: The Berlin Conference of 1884-85 formalized European colonial partition of Africa, ignoring existing African political boundaries.

name: free-education
description: France's Jules Ferry laws established free, compulsory, secular primary education for all children aged 6 to 13.

name: natural-history-museum
description: The Natural History Museum opened in South Kensington, London, housing vast collections of biological and geological specimens.

name: disability-rights
description: Germany's Accident Insurance Law under Bismarck provided compensation for workers disabled by workplace injuries.

name: orphan-support
description: France expanded child welfare legislation, establishing state responsibility for the protection and support of orphaned children.

name: school-lunch-program
description: Sweden pioneered one of the earliest school lunch programs, providing free meals to ensure children's nutrition and school attendance.

name: anti-trust-law
description: The Sherman Anti-Trust Act became the first U.S. federal law to prohibit monopolistic business practices and promote competition.

name: urban-planning
description: The Chicago World's Fair inspired the City Beautiful movement, launching modern urban planning in the United States.

name: xia-dynasty
description: The Xia Dynasty is traditionally considered China's first dynasty, establishing hereditary rule under Yu the Great.

name: traffic-control
description: Early automobile traffic regulations were enacted in the United States as motor vehicles became increasingly common on roads.

name: corporate-tax
description: The United States enacted its first corporate income tax, establishing a 1% tax on net corporate income above $5,000.

name: unemployment-benefits
description: Britain's National Insurance Act created the first compulsory unemployment insurance system, covering certain industries.

name: maternity-leave
description: Britain's National Insurance Act included maternity benefits, providing one of the first state-funded supports for new mothers.

name: public-healthcare-system
description: Bismarck's Health Insurance Act created the world's first national healthcare system, mandating employer-funded worker health insurance.

name: bolshevik-revolution
description: Lenin's Bolsheviks seized power in the October Revolution, establishing the world's first communist state in Russia.

name: great-depression-economy
description: The Wall Street stock market crash triggered the worst global economic depression of the 20th century.

name: child-allowance
description: Belgium introduced one of the first national child allowance systems, providing financial support to families with children.

name: rise-hitler-nazism
description: Adolf Hitler became Chancellor of Germany, rapidly consolidating power and establishing the totalitarian Third Reich.

name: sales-tax
description: U.S. states began adopting general sales taxes during the Depression, with Mississippi being the first in 1930.

name: cuban-missile-crisis-1962
description: A 13-day nuclear standoff between the US and Soviet Union over missiles in Cuba brought the world to the brink of war.

name: fall-berlin-wall-1989
description: The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, symbolizing the end of the Cold War and leading to German reunification.

name: collapse-soviet-union-1991
description: The Soviet Union formally dissolved on December 26, ending the Cold War and creating 15 independent republics.

name: sedentary-hunter-gatherer
description: Hunter-gatherer groups in the Levant and elsewhere began settling permanently, building early villages before the advent of agriculture.

name: norte-chico-caral
description: The Norte Chico civilization at Caral in Peru developed one of the Americas' earliest urban centers, sustained by maritime resources.

name: jomon-maritime-culture
description: Japan's Jomon people developed a sophisticated maritime culture with pottery, fishing, and some of the world's earliest ceramics.
